contents.title: back.jpg
contents.content_html:
1. Alexander, Christopher. Notes
    on the Synthesis of Form. 1964.

  2. Blackwell, Alan F,, et al. Live
    Coding: A User's Manual. MIT
    Press, 2022.

  3. Cache, Bernard. Earth Moves:
    The Furnishing of Territories.
    MIT Press, 1995.

  4. Calvino, Htalo. If on a Winter's
    Night a Traveller. Random House,
    2010.

  5. Cox, Geoff, and Alex McLean.
    Speaking Code: Coding as
    Aesthetic and Political
    Expression. MIT Press, 2012.

  6. “Farshid Moussavi Grounded Visionaries.”
    Philippe Dufour | Invidious,
    yewtu.be/watch?v=aVQ8bnWotEs.

  7. Hackers and-Painters.
    'www.paulgraham.com/hp.html.

  8. Ingold, Tim. “The Textility of Making.”
    Cambridge Journal of Economics, 2009.

i, Farshid. The Function of Form:
Second Edition. Actar, 2018.

  1. Rajchman, John. Constructions. MIT
    Press, 1998. 3

  2. Venezky, Martin. It Is Beautiful--Then
    Gone. Princeton Architectural Press, 2007.
contents.content:
1. Alexander, Christopher. Notes
on the Synthesis of Form. 1964.

2. Blackwell, Alan F,, et al. Live
Coding: A User's Manual. MIT
Press, 2022.

3. Cache, Bernard. Earth Moves:
The Furnishing of Territories.
MIT Press, 1995.

4. Calvino, Htalo. If on a Winter's
Night a Traveller. Random House,
2010.

5. Cox, Geoff, and Alex McLean.
Speaking Code: Coding as
Aesthetic and Political
Expression. MIT Press, 2012.

6. “Farshid Moussavi Grounded Visionaries.”
Philippe Dufour | Invidious,
yewtu.be/watch?v=aVQ8bnWotEs.

7. Hackers and-Painters.
'www.paulgraham.com/hp.html.

8. Ingold, Tim. “The Textility of Making.”
Cambridge Journal of Economics, 2009.

i, Farshid. The Function of Form:
Second Edition. Actar, 2018.

10. Rajchman, John. Constructions. MIT
Press, 1998. 3

11. Venezky, Martin. It Is Beautiful--Then
Gone. Princeton Architectural Press, 2007.

contents.title: intro.jpg
contents.content_html:
---> INTRODUCTION --->Table of contents

--->Part one

---> This publication is a part of an ongoing research project on ---> Something articulated that caught
Ways of Documenting and Talking about one's Work my attention
This second issue is a textual archive trying to bring
together certain ideas that deal with looking at the act of
designing as a performance, and rethinking the process of
design — shifting focus from designing the artifact, to
designing the construction of the artifact.

--->The publication is Part two
typeset in Xanh Mono
and Roboto, both freely |
available on Google
fonts. g : s Writings

o \gs had big impact on how

3 | was thinking

157

\Parnhvee
ULy 5

---> Through the publications, you will see imprints on the
background, smudged writing. These are the visual
artifacts of the tool that this work was conceived in—as
text boxes are moved around the page; they leave behind a
trail of their content in this form, every time the document
is reopened— the trail disappears. Attesting to the influence
of trying to perceive Graphic Design as a preformative N\
practice the tool was built with having the ephemeral B
qualities of performance in mind. “

---> Graphic design then be a
performance?

Furthermore, the arcs and circles form based on the —Flows and effects.

relationship of the proximity of previous and next text box
that exist in the linearity of writing.

And lastly, lines exist.

--->This publication was written and designed in a web based

tool, written in Javascript.
through

The tool was influenced and constructed by the ideas that ) s

bz 7 e W'" dvl‘sc‘uss inhispubicaton: S}.--> Through the process of thinking through the material and
designing it in the tool, the toal has influenced and

o g moulded the interpretations and meanings of the texts.

contents.title: 7.jpg
contents.content_html:
b w“ryr
Part Three = Z
--4-3talo Calvino, in his book 'If en a Winter's Night a Traveller' describes
the book as an entity, alternating between the fiction and traversing
up a level of narration and putting the reader in the scope of the
narration. This book is fiction but it prompts me to think about the
subject object relationship we see in graphic design.

--8-Finding Tim Ingold and his paper, Textility of Making' helped put this
research in place. His articulation of what it means to make, to create
to design— bringing primacy to the process against the outcome—
was what | wanted to communicate. It's been done, articulated and
thought through in a series of papers, books and other writings. Now
what's left is to read it and and let it influence and move my work )
e hene" ">“an ontology that assigns primacy to the
e T ——_ srocesses of formation as against their
final products, and to the flows and
transformations of materials as against
states of matter. Form, to recall Klee's-

words, is death; form-giving is life.”

T want to argue that what Klee said of art is true of
skilled practice in general, namely that it is a question
not of imposing preconceived forms on inert matter but of
intervening in the fields of force and currents of material
wherein forms are gemerated. Practitioners, I comtend, are
wanderers, wayfarers, whose skill lies in their ability to
ﬁnd the grain Of the world’s becoming and to fOllOZt) its the practice of live coding, an act of performing code writing. The

= n 4 _ 0 4 A notion of live coding tries to reframe the creative act as a
course while bending it to their evolving purpose. performance.

--R-Live coding— collaborative book written by multiple authors reviews

--8-5Then o think of graphic design as performing this
exploration, attempting to navigate the flows of material, to
try and construct new forms that provide meaning in given
context but to also surrender to the effects and flows that
the materials exert back on our cognition to allow a
creation of feedback loop that shapes these outcomes.

““This is analogous to the Machinery, a traditional clog
--5-3Speaking Code' by Geoff Coxx and Alex McLean, talk

about what the implications of codes are in our society.
What it means to code. The book plays with interweaving
and inter-playing natural language, speech and code
throughout attempting to draw parallels between the
relationship that speech has to language. "Code is both
script and a performance.”

*>“Programming languages have supplemented natural languages,

suggesting the need for new literacies that include both
natural and artificial languages. These languages form a
“postmodern tower of Babel,” as he [Kittler] puts it, and

this is ohy: “We simply do not know what our writing does.”

Kittler is partly referring to the ways that graphical
interfaces dispense with the need for writing and hide the
“machine” from its users.”

dance originally from the working-class cotton mills of
Lancashire, UK, which mimicked both the sounds and The
Machinery takes the repetitive processes of industrial
technology and acts them out in repetitive, jerky
movements, creating noisy clattering and scrapes with
the clogs, reproducing the sounds and movements of power
Looms and other machinery. ;

By embodying the machine in this dance, the dancer does

not become a machine; rather, machinic movements become

human, and the millworkers regain human agency from
mechanization. Looking at live coding in this Light, we

can _see that live coders similarly do not become coded:, - justmadeuwp
but rather embody code.”

contents.title: 5.jpg
contents.content_html:
Part two

--1-Christopher Alexander’s writings had a big
impact on how | was thinking of graphic
design, this only got me curious on what else

< lis out there. Architecture writing became the
“next stop to figure out my questions.

| currently have 3 books from the MIT Writing Architecture series. I've
been able to read a chapter from 2 of them about 3-5 times— | am
deeply intrigued but not quite sure | completely understand, but | am

excited to try.

--10-Constructions by John Rajchman is responsible for my
excessive use of the word ‘construction’ when talking
about the process of design. The book is a series of
overlapping essays, that suggest connections between-
each other— | really enjoy this style of disconnected
writing as well.

The first chapter, an introduction to the concept of
construction — trying to formulate a loose and
malleable form of constructing. Perhaps a new way to
think about the process of making.

3->Earth Moves, by Bernard Cache—He mentions this book is

an image classifier. A question of what an image even
means. Taking that further to construct and reconstruct
the meaning of these images, where the image is
something constructive, cinematic, pictorial, subjective
and more

--B-Farshid Moussavi, in her talk at Grounded
Visions conference.at Harvard, talks about
rethinking this term 'Function' and how in re
configuring conventions of the function of
places, forms, objects, material and such—
the how of material coming together creates
3 static identity. Here in lies an architects
agency.

“This calls for a transversal design process that hypasses

the traditional top-down or bottom-up approach.

A transversal approach, in which causes and concerns that
are immanent in the enviromment are combined to generate
Jorns, enables us to harness the transformative power of

contemporary reality. Moreover, it enables us to
incorporate greater levels of complexity within built
Jorns, allowing multiple inmputs to imteract simultaneously
on the same plane to gemerate a multitude of novel forms,
each with unique expressions, Semsations and affects.”

“*Bather than trying to deliver something from
nothing through the force of gemius, thinking
tries to construct free spaces within a terrain
bounded by many constraints with the belief
that things will work out in the long run.”

"“The philosopher becomes an experimenter and as
thinking becomes experimentation, its freedom lies
not in pure disinterested automomy but in the power
of it's constructions to go beyond what we already
say, do, are to something singular or untimely”

--9-Farshid Moussavi, in her book ‘The function of form, elaborates on the
relationship between function and form. What's with thinking of function as utility?
Further thinking of what gives form meaning and rethinking the implications of

  • thus form. Moussavi also goes into detail on what it means to design and architect something. Design—a recursive that feeds itself.

This excerpt opens with explaning the that in
Kant's Aesthetic architecture is stated as the
lowest form of art as it is the most tied to
external constraints (economic and physical
limitations) and thus allowing not to think
freely, Rajchman puts these excerpts as
contrast to make a point

contents.title: 3.jpg
contents.content_html:
Part One

| was looking for readings that would help me articulate the
ways | was thinking of design. There was something really
interesting about all these forces that go into the process of
design. How do you articulate these flows of parameters that
are ultimately responsible for the construction of an artifact.

L

I>“The yltimate object of Design is Form. If the
world were totally regular and homogeneous,
there would/ be no forces and mo forms. But an
irregular world tries to compensate for its own
irregularities by fitting itself to them, and
therely ftakes on form.”

--11-Designer as an engineer, or perhaps an architect. The essay
was making a different point, but what it got me at was
thinking of graphic design as architecting and orchestrating
the flow of forces that make up the construction of a design
artifact. | don't yet know what that means.

| borrowed this book from the library— | was heavily into
computational design and | thought-to.myself, if | am so
invested in such a rigid approdchiieed to have-my foot in
the other territory. | thought Venezky's work would be a

\

N\

A
11 Martin Venezky's, Tt is beautiful... then gone' is a beautiful book— filled |\
with essays, conversations, process artifacts and so much more from
Venezky's life, it has has a huge impact on how | think of design and
the process of design.

114n the essay, 'martin venezky, engineer —the nature of his work is
articulated. Something that caught my attention,

: /
i He wants/every product of his

design practice to retain the feel
of handiwork, to be rooted in the

perfect candidate forthat Litledid 1 know,upon reading s ~~¢-angible, unpredictable.realn of

writings and about his process— there was a programmatic
approach at play, the programs were constraints put on by
the chance, indeterminacy, intuition and such. | want
computational design to feel like this. Is that possible?

reality. True to the name of his
firm, Appetite Engineers. he views
his role as that of an engineer.”

Sl

B

/mgay Hackers and Painters— Paul Graham tries to redefine

what it is about computation that he is intrigued by, in doing so
articulating for a lot of people who are interested in making interesting
software (of course, interesting being a subjective term). He draws a
comparison between hackers and painters, trying to elaborate on how
institutions and are current capitalist society often discourages the
t d beautiful concepts of computation. Something that
0 page would be this quote where he tries to
differentiate between the architect an: engineer.
j g
“The border between architecture and engineering is mot sharply
defined, but it's there. It falls-between what and how.
architects decide what to-do, and engineers figure out how to do

i

What and how should not be kept too Separate. You're asking for
trouble if you try to decide what to do without understanding how
to do it. But hacking can certainly be more than just deciding
how to implement some spec. At its best, it's creating the spec--
though it turns out the best way to do that is to implement it.”

Perhaps we as designers have strayed away too much
from the how of the production. The tools we work in are
black boxes, the print shop is a black box, the imprinting
of the printer is a black box. Of course these things can
be learned, but they are seen as different from the
discipline, perhaps if we were closer to the construction
of the artifact, right in the middle of the process, then we
would better be able to.

1-Christopher Alexander's book, ‘Notes on the Synthesis of Form'

1> .achieve fitness between

was recommended to me by Craig Rodmore—I remember two entities. the form in

searching for it on the title in thé @CAD U library and not finding - .y

= o, funnily upon visiting the ibrary, | stumbled uponit..1t___—question and it's context.

was named wrong, 'Notes on a Synthesis of Form' The forfll 'l‘,S the SOLutiOﬂ to
the problem,; the context
defines the problem.”

i

A major part of design then, i$#@ldefine the context, the
forces.

contents.content_html:
Briefed to write and design an annotated bibliography for Ali's Research Methods. This project was designed in a custom web based tool. For a sneak peak of how this tool looks and behaves, check this recording.

The tool has ephemeral qualities of liveness and performance inscribed into it, leaving the marks of text boxes as they move that disappear over time— attempting to re frame graphic design as a performative practice. The arcs make shapes based on the proximity of text boxes to one another. Lines exist.
contents.content:
Briefed to write and design an annotated bibliography for Ali's Research Methods. This project was designed in a custom web based tool. For a sneak peak of how this tool looks and behaves, [check this recording.](https://are.na/block/24747610)

The tool has ephemeral qualities of liveness and performance inscribed into it, leaving the marks of text boxes as they move that disappear over time— attempting to re frame graphic design as a performative practice. The arcs make shapes based on the proximity of text boxes to one another. Lines exist.

contents.title: blood_of_a_god.mp4
contents.description_html: Screen capture of the experimental film web project "Blood of a God" (2023) by Alexandra Maftei. In collaboration with the poet and writer Fira (Cecil Main).
contents.description: Screen capture of the experimental film web project "Blood of a God" (2023) by Alexandra Maftei. In collaboration with the poet and writer Fira (Cecil Main).

embed.html: <iframe class="embedly-embed" src="https://cdn.embedly.com/widgets/media.html?src=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.youtube.com%2Fembed%2FhxzCPr6NAbs%3Fwmode%3Dtransparent%26feature%3Doembed&wmode=transparent&display_name=YouTube&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.youtube.com%2Fwatch%3Fv%3DhxzCPr6NAbs&image=https%3A%2F%2Fi.ytimg.com%2Fvi%2FhxzCPr6NAbs%2Fhqdefault.jpg&key=330ed37aaf0211e083e34040d3dc5c07&type=text%2Fhtml&schema=youtube" width="640" height="360" scrolling="no" title="YouTube embed" frameborder="0" allow="autoplay; fullscreen; encrypted-media; picture-in-picture;" allowfullscreen="true"></iframe>

contents.content_html: I coded a web experience in collaboration with Alexandra Maftei. Alex created a graphic rendering of Cecil Main's poem, 'Blood of a God' using stop motion techniques. We worked to assemble these into an interactive narrative for the web.
contents.content: I coded a web experience in collaboration with [Alexandra Maftei](https://al-xndram.github.io/). Alex created a graphic rendering of Cecil Main's poem, _'Blood of a God'_ using stop motion techniques. We worked to assemble these into an interactive narrative for the web.

contents.content_html: In collaboration with Yigit Toprak. We built the an arduino based mechanical prosthetic to create the Duckinator typeface. Each individual controls only one axis, requiring a build up of coordination amongst the duo.
contents.content: In collaboration with Yigit Toprak. We built the an arduino based mechanical prosthetic to create the Duckinator typeface. Each individual controls only one axis, requiring a build up of coordination amongst the duo.

contents.title: screenshot-2025-10-05-at-2.10.19-pm.png
contents.content_html:
Rethink the book functions in their physical, optical and
psychological aspects. A book has weigh, size, thickness
and tactile qualities, qualitics which are handled by the
hand, as its optical form is handled by the eye ... The
book can be conceived of in the same sense as the handle
of a tool or a utensil .. and with perfect control.*
contents.content:
Rethink the book functions in their physical, optical and
psychological aspects. A book has weigh, size, thickness
and tactile qualities, qualitics which are handled by the
hand, as its optical form is handled by the eye ... The
book can be conceived of in the same sense as the handle
of a tool or a utensil .. and with perfect control.*

contents.content_html:
A visual archive of ways in which one documents their work categorized into three taxonomies. I constructed a modular publication where succeeding pages complete the current page—creating multiple layers of interaction.

Created for Ali Qadeer's Research Methods class.
contents.content:
A visual archive of ways in which one documents their work categorized into three taxonomies. I constructed a modular publication where succeeding pages complete the current page—creating multiple layers of interaction.

Created for Ali Qadeer's Research Methods class.

contents.content_html:
Aryaman Made a search engine for XKCD Comics in GO. He asked me nicely to make a frontend for it. The frontend exposes all of the traffic passing through the client and responses from the server.

The link is offline now.
contents.content:
[Aryaman](https://github.com/aryamancodes/) Made a search engine for XKCD Comics in GO. He asked me nicely to make a frontend for it. The frontend exposes all of the traffic passing through the client and responses from the server.

> The link is offline now.

[Read more about the process here](https://designto.org/blog/all-about-designtos-2024-look-and-feel/)
